model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The budget-aware scheduler hurt badly, so the next step is to return to a constant-rate regime and test a stronger optimizer setup. AdamW with a moderately higher learning rate and slightly stronger decoupled weight decay is a simple, robust change that often improves short-budget CNN training on CIFAR-10.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimizer change produced a solid gain, so the next reasonable step is a small capacity increase rather than a more complex training change. Widening the compact CNN and slightly enlarging the MLP head should improve representation quality within the same short training budget without materially changing the model structure.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, the current run only processes a small fraction of the training set. Increasing batch size is a simple way to expose the model to more examples within the same step budget, which can be especially valuable now that the optimizer and model capacity are in a better regime.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After increasing batch size, the current learning rate is likely conservative for AdamW under the fixed 256-step budget. A moderate LR increase is a simple follow-up that can improve progress per step without changing the model or training loop.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.05","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The higher learning rate regressed, so it makes sense to return to the stronger 1e-3 setting and test a very light amount of dropout. With the wider model and larger batch, a small regularization increase may improve validation loss without materially slowing optimization.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.05","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Light dropout moved validation loss in the wrong direction, so the next useful test is to remove it and push data coverage further. Under a fixed 256-step budget, a moderately larger batch can expose the model to many more training examples without changing the optimizer or architecture.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump from batch size 128 to 192 produced a very large gain, which suggests this workload is strongly limited by example coverage under the fixed 256-step budget. Pushing to batch size 256 is the next simple test to see whether reaching roughly a full epoch of data exposure improves validation loss further.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 320","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch-size scaling has been the clearest source of improvement so far, and the move from 192 to 256 still helped. A further increase to 320 is the simplest next test to see whether additional data coverage within the fixed 256-step budget continues to outweigh the larger per-step compute.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 320","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move to batch size 320 overshot, but the strong gains from larger batches suggest the best regime is still near 256. Reverting to 256 and testing a modest LR increase is a simple way to check whether the larger-batch setting was slightly underpowered at 1e-3 without repeating the clearly worse 1.5e-3 jump.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current optimization regime is much stronger than the original baseline, so the next promising step is a modest capacity increase rather than another large training change. Increasing the base channel count keeps the architecture simple while giving the model more representational power under a setting that now appears capable of using it.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The cosine schedule regressed sharply, so the next step is to restore the stronger constant-LR setup and test slightly stronger regularization. With batch size 256 and a higher learning rate, increasing AdamW weight decay is a simple way to probe whether the current best regime is a bit under-regularized.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The higher weight decay did not beat the previous best, so the next useful optimizer-side test is to restore the stronger regularization setting and shorten AdamW's second-moment horizon. A lower beta2 can help a short-budget run react faster to fresh gradients, which is often beneficial when training lasts only 256 steps.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Lowering AdamW beta2 to 0.99 helped a bit, which suggests the current optimizer setup may tolerate a slightly more aggressive step size. A small LR increase is the simplest next probe of that regime without changing the model, batch size, or regularization.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.98)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The recent gains came from making AdamW respond more quickly within the short 256-step budget. Lowering beta2 one more notch is a focused follow-up to test whether an even shorter second-moment horizon improves adaptation further in this fixed-step regime.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.98)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Pushing beta2 lower hurt, so the next step is to return to the stronger optimizer setting and try a cheap capacity increase in the classifier head. Expanding the hidden layer is much less expensive than widening the convolutional trunk and may still improve how the 2-block feature extractor is used.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The larger classifier head did not help, so it makes sense to return to the stronger baseline structure and try a lighter regularization change instead. Mild label smoothing is a simple, low-overhead way to improve calibration and validation loss on CIFAR-10 without changing the optimizer or budget usage.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.35e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Label smoothing did not help, so the best-known setup is still the unsmoothed loss with the faster AdamW variant. With only a small gain from moving to 1.3e-3, the most efficient final-direction check is a very slight LR increase around the current best regime.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.35e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The most recent change still improved validation loss, so the strongest last-step bet is to continue the same successful direction with one more small learning-rate increase. This keeps the experiment simple and stays close to the best-known regime rather than branching into a qualitatively different idea on the final move.","secondary_modes":[]}
